data.content_ref: inline:The account posits a pandemic sweeping through densely populated palatial centers as the primary cause of collapse, yet it excludes Egypt on the grounds of its more dispersed population and lower dependency on centralized palace complexes. This contradicts the well-documented fact that Egypt during the New Kingdom had major dense urban and palatial centers (e.g., Pi-Ramesses, Thebes) with populations comparable to other Eastern Mediterranean cities, and its bureaucracy and grain storage were highly centralized. The account offers no evidence that Egypt's population distribution or palace dependency was structurally different enough to halt a pathogen that supposedly decimated every other urbanized, trade-connected polity in the region.
